_note: Real-world estimation facts for the Fermi market-making mode. Every trueValue below was checked against a primary/credible source (national surveys, official measurement bodies, Wikipedia, Britannica, NASA/Planck, peer-reviewed studies, or original shipbuilder records) as of 2026-07. A few figures are genuinely contested across sources rather than settled — those are called out explicitly in the question's own hints (e.g. blood vessel length, neuron count, Mariana Trench depth) rather than presented as more precise than they are.

questions.prompt: Total length of the Great Wall of China, including all branches and trenches
hints: It's measured in thousands of kilometers, not hundreds.
It's roughly 5x the driving distance across the continental United States (~4,300 km).
The main wall alone (excluding branches) is about 8,850 km; branches add well over half again as much.
China's 2012 state survey put the total length, including all sections and branches, at just over 21,000 km.

questions.prompt: Height of the Eiffel Tower, including its broadcast antenna
hints: It's measured in tens to low hundreds of meters, not thousands.
It's more than double the height of the Great Pyramid of Giza (~139 m).
Without its antenna, the structure itself is 300 m tall.
With the antenna added in recent years, the official height is 330 m.

questions.prompt: Height of Mount Everest above sea level
hints: It's measured in thousands of meters.
It's roughly 1.4x the height of Denali (~6,190 m), North America's tallest peak.
It's just under 9,000 meters.
The 2020 China-Nepal joint survey fixed the official height at 8,849 m.

questions.prompt: Average Earth-to-Moon distance (center to center)
hints: It's measured in hundreds of thousands of kilometers.
Light takes about 1.3 seconds to cover this distance.
It's roughly 30 Earth diameters.
The average center-to-center distance is about 384,400 km.

questions.prompt: Empty (operating) weight of a Boeing 747-400, no fuel or payload
hints: It's measured in tens to low hundreds of thousands of kilograms.
It weighs roughly as much as 30 fully grown blue whales.
Empty, it's just under 200,000 kg.
The operating empty weight of a 747-400 is about 183,500 kg.

questions.prompt: Number of floors in the Burj Khalifa
hints: It's measured in the hundreds, not thousands.
It has more than twice as many floors as the Empire State Building (102).
It has more than 150 floors.
The Burj Khalifa has 163 floors.

questions.prompt: Speed of sound at sea level, dry air, 20°C
hints: It's measured in the hundreds of meters per second.
It's roughly 1/875,000th the speed of light.
It's a bit faster than 300 m/s.
At sea level in dry air at 20°C, sound travels at about 343 m/s.

questions.prompt: Number of bones in an adult human skeleton
hints: It's measured in the low hundreds.
Babies are born with about 300 bones; many fuse together by adulthood.
It's fewer than 250.
The adult human skeleton has 206 bones.

questions.prompt: Length of the Suez Canal
hints: It's measured in the hundreds of kilometers, not thousands.
It's roughly 4-5x the length of the Panama Canal (~82 km).
It's just under 200 km.
The Suez Canal is about 193 km long.

questions.prompt: Depth of the Challenger Deep, the deepest known point in the ocean
hints: It's measured in thousands of meters — deeper than Everest is tall.
It's the deepest known point in any ocean on Earth.
It's just under 11,000 meters deep.
Modern surveys put it at approximately 10,935 m, though estimates vary by a few dozen meters across studies.

questions.prompt: Height of the Great Pyramid of Giza today (it was taller when built)
hints: It's measured in the hundreds of meters, similar order of magnitude to a modern skyscraper.
It's less than half the height of the Eiffel Tower (330 m).
When built, it stood about 146.6 m tall — erosion and lost casing stones have shaved off a few meters since.
Today it stands at approximately 138.5 meters.

questions.prompt: Height of the CN Tower in Toronto
hints: It's measured in the hundreds of meters.
It was the tallest freestanding structure in the world for over 30 years (1975-2007), until the Burj Khalifa surpassed it.
It's taller than the Eiffel Tower (330 m) by more than 200 m.
The CN Tower stands 553.33 meters tall.

questions.prompt: Total height of Angel Falls, the world's tallest waterfall
hints: It's measured in the hundreds of meters — taller than any building on Earth.
It's more than 15x the height of Niagara Falls' Horseshoe Falls (~51 m).
Its uninterrupted plunge alone is about 807 m; the total height including lower cascades runs higher.
Angel Falls has a total height of about 979 meters.

questions.prompt: Length of the Panama Canal (deep water to deep water)
hints: It's measured in the tens of kilometers, not hundreds.
It's less than half the length of the Suez Canal (193 km).
It's a bit less than 100 km.
The Panama Canal is about 82 km long, from deep water in the Atlantic to deep water in the Pacific.

questions.prompt: Total length of the Channel Tunnel (Eurotunnel) connecting England and France
hints: It's measured in the tens of kilometers.
About 38 km of it runs under the seabed of the English Channel.
It's shorter than the Panama Canal (82 km).
The Channel Tunnel is about 50.45 km long in total.

questions.prompt: Maximum depth of Lake Baikal, the world's deepest lake
hints: It's measured in the thousands of meters — deeper than most lakes by a wide margin.
It holds more fresh water than all five North American Great Lakes combined.
It's over 1,600 meters deep at its deepest point.
Lake Baikal's maximum depth is about 1,642 meters.

questions.prompt: Height of Niagara Falls' Horseshoe Falls
hints: It's measured in the tens of meters, not hundreds.
It's less than a twentieth of the height of Angel Falls (979 m).
It's a bit higher than 50 m.
The Horseshoe Falls is commonly cited at about 51 meters tall.

questions.prompt: Height of the Statue of Liberty, from ground to the tip of the torch
hints: It's measured in the tens to low hundreds of meters.
The pedestal alone is almost as tall as the copper statue itself.
It's just under 100 meters.
Ground to torch tip, the Statue of Liberty measures about 93 meters.

questions.prompt: Average distance from Earth to the Sun
hints: It's on the order of a hundred million kilometers — light takes several minutes to cross it.
This distance defines 1 astronomical unit (AU), the standard yardstick for distances in the solar system.
It's just under 150 million km.
The average Earth-Sun distance is about 149.6 million km (1 AU).

questions.prompt: Speed of light in a vacuum
hints: It's measured in the hundreds of thousands of kilometers per second.
It's often rounded to 300,000 km/s for quick estimates.
It's an exactly defined constant since 1983 — the meter is now defined in terms of it.
The speed of light is exactly 299,792.458 km/s.

questions.prompt: Diameter of the Moon
hints: It's measured in the thousands of kilometers.
It's about 1/4 the diameter of Earth.
It's a bit less than the width of the contiguous United States.
The Moon's diameter is about 3,476 km.

questions.prompt: Circumference of the Earth at the equator
hints: It's measured in the tens of thousands of kilometers.
A commercial jet flying nonstop at cruising speed would take roughly two days to cover this distance.
It's just over 40,000 km.
Earth's equatorial circumference is about 40,075 km.

questions.prompt: Time it takes sunlight to travel from the Sun and reach Earth
hints: It's a matter of minutes, not hours.
It's roughly the Earth-Sun distance (149.6 million km) divided by the speed of light (~300,000 km/s).
It's a bit over 8 minutes.
Sunlight takes about 500 seconds (8 minutes 20 seconds) to reach Earth.

questions.prompt: Age of the universe since the Big Bang
hints: It's measured in billions of years, not millions.
It's roughly 3x the age of the Earth itself (~4.5 billion years).
It's just under 14 billion years.
The current best estimate, from Planck satellite data, is 13.8 billion years.

questions.prompt: Distance to Proxima Centauri, the nearest star to the Sun
hints: It's measured in single-digit light years.
Even at the speed of light, a signal would take over 4 years to get there.
It's a bit over 4 light years.
Proxima Centauri is about 4.25 light years away.

questions.prompt: Number of neurons in the adult human brain
hints: It's measured in the tens of billions.
For decades, textbooks cited a round '100 billion' — that figure was never rigorously measured.
It's under 90 billion.
A widely cited 2009 count puts it at about 86 billion neurons (recent work argues the true range may be anywhere from roughly 61 to 99 billion).

questions.prompt: Total length of all blood vessels in the human body, laid end to end (the classic textbook estimate)
hints: It's measured in the tens of thousands of kilometers — more than twice Earth's circumference.
Most of the length comes from capillaries, the body's smallest vessels, not the large arteries and veins.
The classic figure (from an early-1900s calculation) is around 100,000 km — modern capillary-only estimates actually run lower, closer to 10,000-20,000 km.
The textbook number most often quoted in Fermi-estimation interviews is about 100,000 km.

questions.prompt: Number of times an average adult heart beats in a day, at a resting rate of about 70 bpm
hints: It's measured in the hundreds of thousands.
70 beats/minute × 60 minutes × 24 hours gets you most of the way there.
It's just over 100,000.
At a resting rate of about 70 bpm, the heart beats roughly 100,000 times per day.

questions.prompt: Average weight of an adult blue whale
hints: It's measured in the tens of thousands of kilograms — heavier than any dinosaur is confirmed to have been.
It's roughly as heavy as 15-20 adult African elephants combined.
Averages across populations range from about 70,000 to 130,000 kg depending on sex and region.
A commonly cited average for an adult blue whale is about 100,000 kg (100 tonnes).

questions.prompt: Gestation period of an African elephant
hints: It's measured in the hundreds of days — the longest gestation of any land mammal.
It's close to twice the length of a human pregnancy (~280 days).
It's roughly 22 months.
African elephant gestation averages about 656 days (typically cited as a 640-673 day range).

questions.prompt: Height of Hyperion, the world's tallest known living tree (a coast redwood)
hints: It's measured in the hundreds of meters — taller than most office towers.
It's a coast redwood growing in Redwood National Park, California.
It's just over 115 meters tall.
Hyperion measures about 116 meters.

questions.prompt: Displacement (fully-loaded weight) of the RMS Titanic
hints: It's measured in the tens of thousands of tonnes.
A commonly repeated myth claims 66,000 tons for Titanic's displacement — that figure is incorrect.
It's a bit above 50,000 tonnes.
Titanic's actual displacement, per original shipbuilder records, was about 52,310 tonnes.

questions.prompt: Number of rivets used to construct the Eiffel Tower
hints: It's measured in the millions.
The tower is built from about 18,000 individual iron pieces, each joined by several rivets.
It's a bit over 2 million.
The Eiffel Tower was assembled using about 2.5 million rivets.

questions.prompt: Number of steps from the ground to the top of the Eiffel Tower
hints: It's measured in the low thousands.
It would take a fit visitor roughly 30-45 minutes to climb them all (though most people take the elevator).
It's over 1,600 steps.
There are 1,665 steps from ground level to the top of the Eiffel Tower.

questions.prompt: Length of the Trans-Siberian Railway, from Moscow to Vladivostok
hints: It's measured in the thousands of kilometers — the longest single railway line in the world.
The journey crosses eight time zones and takes about a week to complete.
It's just over 9,000 km.
The Trans-Siberian Railway spans about 9,289 km.

questions.prompt: Total weight of the Great Pyramid of Giza
hints: It's measured in the millions of tonnes.
It's built from roughly 2.3 million stone blocks, averaging about 2.5 tonnes each.
It's just under 6 million tonnes.
Estimates put the Great Pyramid's total weight at approximately 5.9 million tonnes.

questions.prompt: Area of the Sahara Desert
hints: It's measured in the millions of square kilometers — continent-sized.
It's comparable in area to the entire United States.
It's a bit over 9 million km².
The Sahara covers about 9.2 million km², making it the largest hot desert in the world.
